title: Soy Sauce on Linen
timeEstimate: 15–35 minutes; cold flush plus salt-rinse, structured linen = pro
stainChemistry: Soy sauce is dark brown caramel color plus salt plus the tannin-like browning compounds from fermentation. The salt is the catch — it acts as a fixing agent that drives the brown deeper into the fiber the longer it sits, which is why the standard advice on soy sauce is 'rinse fast, cold.' On linen (cellulose, similar to cotton), the protocol works well IF the linen is simple flat washable. Structured / lined / dry-clean-only / vintage linen narrows the bench because linen tolerates less aggressive handling than cotton when wet. The two hard lines: NO hot water (sets the brown plus heat-cross-links the tannin into linen) and NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — alkaline darkens tannin permanently into a deeper brown.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flush rinses out the salt before it can set the color, then dilutes the loose pigment. Mild acid (white vinegar) keeps the tannin on the lighter side of its color range and disrupts the tannin-fiber grip. 3% peroxide on white linen oxidizes residual brown. Cool wash plus air-dry locks nothing. The recurring failure is reaching for OxiClean alone (alkaline) before peroxide — locks the tannin into permanent brown.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): soy sauce is melanoidins from fermentation Maillard browning + salt + amino acids + phenolic tannin. Salt acts as a mordant, fixing chromophore to cellulose. Pro uses cold acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) plus 3% H₂O₂ for residual chromophore plus acetic acid neutralizing rinse.
defaultAssumption: Sushi night / takeout splash on a simple flat linen napkin or tablecloth, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Structured / lined / dry-clean-only / vintage linen = pro. Hot-dried = often permanent.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen = pro from minute one. Simple flat washable linen = home protocol candidate. If hot-dried, expectations drop sharply.

spottingProtocol.technique: Rinse salt before it sets color
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white catch towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath. 60–90 seconds. Cold only — heat sets the brown into linen. Salt rinses out fast at the start; if you wait, salt fixes the color deeper.

spottingProtocol.technique: Mild acid keeps tannin on light side
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tsp white vinegar + 4 tbsp cool water on cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dab diluted vinegar on the spot, 3–5 min. Disrupts tannin grip and keeps it on the lighter side of its color range. Hidden test on dyed linen first — vinegar can lighten some dyes.
WHITE or light linen only. Hidden test on dyed linen. 3% peroxide damp cloth, 5–10 min, blot. Oxidizes residual brown chromophore. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY FLAT — linen wrinkles aggressively when wet, hanging stretches it. Inspect dry before any iron. NEVER hot dryer until cleared.

steps: 1. Care label + structure check — structured / vintage = pro.
2. Cold water back-flush 60–90 sec — rinse salt before it fixes color.
3. Diluted white vinegar dab, 3–5 min — keeps tannin on light side.
4. 3% H₂O₂ for white linen, 5–10 min — hidden test on dyed.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY flat. No heat until cleared.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — heat sets brown plus cross-links tannin on linen
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets brown plus cross-links tannin into linen
NEVER alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) — locks tannin permanently darker
Speed matters — salt fixes color the longer you wait
Structured / lined / vintage linen = pro from minute one
Hidden test on dyed linen before vinegar and peroxide
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

homeSolutions.agent: Speed Beats Chemistry — Salt Is the Catch
homeSolutions.instruction: Soy sauce has a lot of salt, and salt fixes the brown color into the fabric the longer it sits. So the protocol leads with a fast cold-water rinse, not chemistry. Two hard rules: NO hot water (sets the brown plus cross-links tannin into linen) and NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide) — alkaline locks tannin into a permanent darker brown.

Dry-clean-only linen, structured pieces (lined jackets, drapery), or vintage linen all narrow the bench sharply — that's pro from minute one. Simple flat washable linen (napkin, plain shirt, tablecloth) = home candidate.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush From the Back — 60 to 90 Seconds
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath the front. Let the water push the soy sauce out the way it came in. 60 to 90 seconds. Cold only. The salt rinses out fast at the beginning of the rinse — if you wait or use warm water, salt fixes the color deeper into the linen.

Mix 1 teaspoon white vinegar with 4 tablespoons cool water. Dab on the spot, let sit 3 to 5 minutes, blot. Vinegar keeps the tannin on the lighter side of its color range and disrupts the grip on the linen fiber. Hidden-area test on dyed linen first — vinegar can lighten some dyes.

WHITE or very light linen only: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Oxidizes residual brown. Hidden-area test on dyed linen first — skip if color transfers.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners (baking soda, OxiClean) — toxic fumes plus locks tannin.

homeSolutions.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY FLAT — linen wrinkles aggressively when wet, and hanging it can stretch it. Inspect dry before any iron. NEVER hot dryer until the spot is fully cleared. Heat at this step locks any residual brown.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO alkaline cleaners (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone before peroxide).
3. Cold water back-flush 60–90 sec — speed beats chemistry.
4. Diluted white vinegar dab, 3–5 min — hidden test on dyed linen.
5. 3% peroxide for white linen, 5–10 min — hidden test on dyed.
6. Cool wash + AIR-DRY flat. No iron until cleared.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen (pro). Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent). Stop if alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) was already applied (tannin locked). Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets brown plus cross-links tannin
NEVER alkaline cleaners — locks tannin permanently darker
Speed matters — salt fixes color over time
Structured / vintage linen = pro from minute one
Hidden test on dyed linen before vinegar and peroxide
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never hot water on soy sauce + linen
Never alkaline cleaners on tannin — locks color permanently darker
Never iron the stained area before clearing
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline
fiberSensitivities: White or light washable linen: tolerates vinegar + 3% peroxide
Dyed linen: hidden test mandatory before vinegar and peroxide
Structured / lined linen: pro — warps under wet handling
Vintage / antique linen: pro — fiber fragility
Linen wrinkles aggressively wet — air-dry FLAT, not hanging

materialWarnings: Salt fixes color — speed beats chemistry on soy sauce
No hot water / no hot dryer — heat sets brown into linen
No alkaline cleaners — locks tannin permanently darker
Structured / vintage linen = pro from minute one

escalation.when: Dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen. Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent). Alkaline cleaner already applied (tannin locked permanently darker). 2 home rounds plateau. Hidden-area test fails on dyed linen.
escalation.whatToTell: "Soy sauce on [white / dyed / structured / vintage] linen, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cold back-flush + diluted vinegar + 3% peroxide on white + cool wash + air-dry flat]. No hot, no alkaline. [Residual brown / hot-dried suspected / alkaline used / structured piece]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with linen experience plus tannin-spotter chemistry

contextQuestions: Simple flat linen, structured / lined, or vintage?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has alkaline cleaner (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean alone) been used?
Light or dark soy sauce (dark soy is more concentrated)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Salt-fixing means speed beats chemistry — every minute that passes makes the stain harder. Dark / aged soy sauce concentrate is harder than light soy. Structured linen needs pro because the wet handling that linen needs for tannin removal warps lined or structured pieces. The most common home failure is reaching for OxiClean alone (alkaline) which locks tannin into permanent darker brown.
